file_name: PSGT Agreement NDA New Supplier Non Disclosure Template DC.txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall (and shall procure that each of its relevant affiliates shall), and shall use reasonable endeavors to procure that any third party subcontractors shall, comply with their respective obligations under all applicable law regarding data protection in relation to all Personal Data and Confidential Information (together, “Protected Data”) that is processed by it in the course of performing its obligations under this Agreemen t, and take such steps as are reasonably required by the other Party to ensure that the other Party is able to comply with such law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should (and should procure that each of its relevant affiliates should), and shall use its best judgment to procure that any third party subcontractors shall, endeavor to comply with their obligations under applicable law regarding data protection in relation to all Personal Data and Confidential Information (together, “Protected Data”) that is processed by it in the course of performing its obligations under this Agreement, and take steps they deem necessary to ensure compliance with such law.
perturbation.explanation: The change from "shall comply" to "endeavor to comply" and "should procure" weakens the obligation to comply with data protection laws. Replacing "steps as are reasonably required" with "steps they deem necessary" introduces subjective discretion. This ambiguity contradicts the strict requirements of data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1) of the GDPR requires that personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner. The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to strictly comply with data protection law, potentially leading to unlawful processing of personal data because the term 'endeavor to comply' and 'steps they deem necessary' may not align with GDPR's stringent requirements. It's also weakening the obligation to procure third-party subcontractor compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with data protection laws by changing "shall comply" to "endeavor to comply" and "steps as are reasonably required" to "steps they deem necessary." This creates ambiguity and introduces subjective discretion, potentially leading to non-compliance with data protection laws. The scraped law snippet from Art. 5 GDPR states that personal data "shall be processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner." By weakening the compliance obligation, the modified text contradicts this requirement, as 'endeavor to comply' does not ensure lawful processing as mandated by the GDPR.

perturbation.original_text: The Supplier agrees that it may only access and interact with any Confidential Information made available to it to the extent necessary for it to perform the Services. The Supplier further agrees that, as a recipient of Confidential Information: (i) except as expressly permitted by an applicable Services Agreement, it shall not, for any purpose, use, disclose, reproduce, transfer or reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Informa tion
perturbation.changed_text: The Supplier agrees that it may access and interact with Confidential Information made available to it. The Supplier further agrees that, as a recipient of Confidential Information: (i) except as permitted by an applicable Services Agreement, it will try not to use, disclose, reproduce, transfer or reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information
perturbation.explanation: Removing "only to the extent necessary" broadens the scope of permissible access. Replacing "shall not" with "will try not to" weakens the prohibition on using, disclosing, etc., confidential information. This creates ambiguity as to what constitutes acceptable use and disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. CPLR § 4548 protects trade secrets. The change weakens the protection afforded to confidential information and trade secrets by allowing broader access and making non-disclosure discretionary, potentially enabling misappropriation. "Will try not to" lacks a definite legal standard and could allow for unauthorized use and disclosure.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the supplier access confidential information "only to the extent necessary" and "shall not" disclose it, except as permitted. The changed text broadens access and weakens the prohibition to "will try not to," creating ambiguity. The law explanation states this lacks a "definite legal standard" and could allow unauthorized use. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define 'trade secrets' or detail specific regulations against weakened confidentiality clauses, the law explanation, combined with the removal of the restriction and the weakening of the prohibition, indicates that the change undermines the protection afforded to confidential information, creating a contradiction. The changed text creates a non-compliance issue.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of the Services for any reas on or at the written request of a Disclosing Party at any time, the Supplier shall destroy (and promptly deliver a certification of such destruction) or promptly deliver to each Disclosing Party, or to the requesting Disclosing Party as the case may be, all tangible embodiments of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in the Supplier’s or its employees’ possession, including but no t limited to copies, notes, packages, pictures, diagrams, computer memory media and all other tangible embodiments of, or materials containing, any portion of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of the Services for any reason or at the request of a Disclosing Party, the Supplier should endeavor to destroy or deliver to each Disclosing Party, all tangible embodiments of such Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in the Supplier’s possession.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall destroy" to "should endeavor to destroy" makes the destruction of confidential information discretionary. Removing "promptly deliver a certification of such destruction" removes accountability. Removing the phrase  "in the Supplier’s or its employees’ possession, including but not limited to copies, notes, packages, pictures, diagrams, computer memory media and all other tangible embodiments of, or materials containing, any portion of the Confidential Information" limits the application of the clause, creating ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to destroy or return confidential information upon termination, the modified text increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation. The lack of a requirement to certify destruction and the ambiguity around the scope of covered materials creates a loophole where trade secrets could be retained and potentially used unlawfully.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially leading to trade secret misappropriation, and contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1832. The original text ensured destruction or return of confidential information upon termination, creating a clear obligation, while the modified text introduces ambiguity by changing "shall destroy" to "should endeavor to destroy", making the destruction discretionary and removing accountability by removing the certification requirement. The law snippet provided refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which includes provisions related to the theft of trade secrets. The changes in the contract language weaken the protection of confidential information and increase the risk of misappropriation, creating a contradiction. The removal of 'promptly deliver a certification of such destruction' eliminates a means of accountability and the ambiguity introduced weakens the obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Further, the Data Protection Laws make it mand atory that CGSH obtain contractual guarantees from all third parties who will be given access to or who become aware of Protected Data during the course of their visits to the Premises in or der that they may undertake the role of a service provider. The Data Protection Laws also require CGSH to ensure they have measures in place to prevent the unauthorized or unlawful use of Pr otected Data and to prevent accidental loss, destruction or damage to any personal information. The Supplier must therefore ensure, by way of written contract between it and its employees, subcontractors and individuals visiting the Premises on the Supplier’s behalf (each a “Contractor” and, toget her, the “Contractors”), that if at any time a Contractor becomes aware of Protected Data the Su pplier or its Contractors will not (i) share this information with anyone outside of CGSH, (ii) take any information away from the Premises, and/or (iii) duplicate or copy any Protected Data.
perturbation.changed_text: Further, the Data Protection Laws suggest that CGSH obtain some form of guarantees from third parties who may be given access to Protected Data. The Data Protection Laws encourage CGSH to consider implementing measures to prevent the unauthorized use of Protected Data. The Supplier should therefore consider ensuring, via contract, that if a Contractor becomes aware of Protected Data the Supplier or its Contractors try not to (i) share this information with anyone outside of CGSH, (ii) take any information away from the Premises, and/or (iii) duplicate or copy any Protected Data.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications replace mandatory requirements with suggestions and encouragement. "Make it mandatory" becomes "Data Protection Laws suggest," "ensure" becomes "consider implementing measures," and "must therefore ensure" becomes "should therefore consider ensuring." The phrases "try not to" weakens the prohibition against sharing, taking, or copying data. This creates ambiguity and makes compliance discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Schedule 11 of the Data Protection Act 2018 requires controllers to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The changes weaken the obligations to protect data, replacing mandatory requirements with suggestions. The move from 'ensure' to 'consider ensuring' and 'try not to' contravenes the necessity for strict and enforced security measures under the DPA 2018. While the original text makes reference to the Data Protection Act 1998 and the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46/EC, the 2018 law is now the governing law.

Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Beta This part of GOV.UK is being rebuilt – find out what beta means IHTM12101 - Succession: Intestacy: Background Intestacy arises where a deceased person does not effectively dispose of their property by Will ( IHTM12041 ) There is either: a total intestacy where none of the estate is disposed of, or a partial intestacy where part of the estate is not disposed of. There are legal rules ( IHTM12111 ) for the distribution of an estate on intestacy. The rules in England & Wales differ from those in Scotland ( IHTM12142 ) and Northern Ireland ( IHTM12162 ) Print this page Is this page useful? perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates certain actions regarding data protection, while the changed contract weakens these requirements by using suggestive language like 'suggest,' 'consider implementing,' and 'try not to.' This directly contradicts the intent of data protection laws, which typically require controllers to implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, as suggested by the Law Explanation referencing Schedule 11 of the Data Protection Act 2018. The scraped snippets don't provide explicit contradicting text, but the essence of the change creates a legal contradiction by undermining data protection obligations.
